patterns: should i lift heavy or light
is full body or split better
how long should a workout be
what's the best way to train
responses: For strength, stick to 3–6 reps with heavier loads. For muscle size, 8–12 reps with moderate loads works best. Add a little weight or a rep each week — that's progressive overload.
Train each muscle group 2x per week, do 3–4 working sets per exercise, and chase progressive overload week to week. Quality reps beat junk volume every time.
Stay consistent with 3–5 sessions a week, prioritize compound lifts (squat, deadlift, press, row), and add weight or reps gradually. Sleep and food do half the work.
Form first, then load. Slow eccentric, controlled concentric, full range of motion. Add weight only when reps stay clean.
{firstName}, your plan already structures this for you. Focus on hitting today's exercises with good form and adding 1 rep or a small weight bump each week.
Compound lifts (squats, deadlifts, presses, rows) build the most muscle. Spend 70% of your time there, 30% on accessories.
Train, eat, sleep. Skip any of those three and progress stalls. Aim for 7–9h of sleep on training days.

patterns: how much protein do i need
how many calories should i eat
what's a good post workout meal
what should i eat for breakfast
responses: Aim for around 1.6–2.2g of protein per kg of bodyweight, eat enough carbs to fuel your sessions, and don't be afraid of healthy fats. Whole foods first.
Pre-workout: carbs + a little protein 1–2 hours before. Post-workout: protein + carbs within a couple hours. Hit your daily protein target and the rest sorts itself out.
For fat loss, run a small calorie deficit (~300–500 kcal) and keep protein high to protect muscle. For muscle gain, a small surplus + progressive overload is the recipe.
Creatine monohydrate (3–5g/day) is the most evidence-backed supplement. Whey protein is convenient if you struggle to hit protein from food. Most other supplements aren't necessary.
Build meals around: a protein (chicken, fish, eggs, tofu, beans), a carb (rice, oats, potato, bread), veggies, and a fat (oil, nuts, avocado). Repeat with variety.
{firstName}, your goal is {goal} — match your calories to that. Fat loss = small deficit, muscle gain = small surplus, maintenance = match expenditure.
Hydration matters too — aim for 30–40 ml of water per kg of bodyweight on training days.

patterns: i don't feel like working out
responses: You don't have to feel like it — you just have to start. Promise yourself 10 minutes; you'll usually finish the session.
Future you is already proud of present you for showing up. Even a light session beats zero. Let's go, {firstName}.
Discipline beats motivation. Lace up, do the warm-up, and decide afterwards. You've got this.
{firstName}, you've already done {totalWorkouts} workouts — momentum is yours. Don't break the chain.
Your {streak}-day streak is on the line. Even a 15-minute session keeps it alive.
Hard days build the most. Show up, do less than usual if you must, but show up.
Pick the easiest exercise on today's list and just start there. Most of the time the rest follows.

patterns: should i take a rest day
do i need a rest day
what to do on rest days
how many rest days per week
is it okay to skip a workout
responses: Rest is where the gains happen. Aim for at least 1–2 full rest days per week. On those days, light walking or mobility work is great.
If you're really sore or sleep was poor, take the day off or do active recovery — easy walk, stretching, mobility. Your next session will be better for it.
Recovery hinges on sleep (7–9h), protein, hydration, and easy movement on off days. Don't grind through real fatigue.
Soreness is normal up to ~48h. Sharp or one-sided pain is not — that's a stop sign.
Active recovery: 20–30 min easy walk, foam rolling, mobility flow. Skip anything that spikes your heart rate.
Every 6–8 weeks consider a deload — drop weights to ~60% for a week. You'll come back stronger.
{firstName}, your {daysPerWeek}-day plan already has rest built in. Trust it.

patterns: i think i hurt my shoulder
responses: Sorry to hear that. I'm not qualified to diagnose injuries — please pause training the affected area and consult a doctor or physiotherapist before pushing through.
That sounds like something to get checked by a professional. Rest it, ice if it's acute, and book a physio. Don't train through pain.
I can't give medical advice. The safe move is to stop loading the area, see a qualified physio or doctor, and ease back in only when they clear you.
Ache is one thing, sharp pain is another. If it's sharp, swelling, or affects daily life — see a professional ASAP.
Train around it, not through it. Pick exercises that don't load the painful area and book a physio for proper diagnosis.
I'm not a doctor. For anything beyond mild post-workout soreness, please get professional input before training the area again.

patterns: what if i only have dumbbells
can i do this at home
what to do with just bodyweight
can i do this without weights
responses: Most barbell lifts have solid dumbbell or bodyweight versions. Push-ups for bench, goblet squats for back squats, single-leg RDLs for deadlifts, rows with bands or dumbbells for pulls.
Update your equipment in onboarding and I'll regenerate a plan that fits what you actually have. In the meantime, bodyweight + dumbbells covers 90% of the basics.
Adapt freely: swap the lift for one that hits the same primary muscle with the gear you've got. The pattern (push, pull, hinge, squat, carry) matters more than the tool.
{firstName}, your equipment is set to {equipment}. Want to change it? Update it in Settings and I'll suggest matching exercises.
Push-ups, pike push-ups, dips, rows, rear-delt flies with bands — you can hit every upper body muscle without a barbell. Want me to walk through it?
Bodyweight squats, lunges, single-leg work, glute bridges, and Nordic curls cover lower body without any equipment.

responses: Open the Workout tab, tap Log, fill in duration, and hit Save — it'll write to your history and award XP.
From the Home or Workout tab you can run today's session, or use Log to add a manual entry. Both update your dashboard.
Use the Log screen for manual entries (cardio, classes, anything off-plan). Sessions you run from the plan log themselves on completion.
Tap the Log button on the Workout tab to add anything — runs, classes, yoga, anything. It all earns XP and counts toward your streak.
Sessions you complete via the Run screen save automatically. For manual entries (off-plan stuff), use the Log button.
Past workouts live on the Dashboard tab — scroll down for the history list.

responses: Most account/profile stuff lives in the Settings tab. Tap your avatar at the top-right or use the Settings tab in the bottom bar.
Goals and equipment can be updated in Settings → Edit Profile. Changes feed into your next plan regeneration.
Your achievements are on the Dashboard tab — tap the Level pill to see all unlocks.
Plan regeneration: Settings → Regenerate Plan. Or change goal/equipment first and the next plan picks them up.
Calendar is on the Workout tab — scroll down to see your planned days.
Notifications: Settings → Notifications. Soft prompts ask you first, then iOS/Android dialogs.
